summary: Julia Evans explains her decision to switch from giving away her programming zines for free to selling them for $10 each. She weighs the tradeoff between free distribution (which maximizes reach) and paid distribution (which funds higher quality work and signals seriousness). Using her own data, she argues that 3,000 paid sales may result in more actual readers than 5,000 free downloads, since buyers are more likely to read what they paid for. She discusses corporate pricing experiments, the advantages of posting drafts on Twitter as a free complement, and various friction points she's encountered with selling digital products.
key_points: Free distribution maximizes reach, but paid distribution funds investment in quality and sustains motivation to do the work well
3,000 sales may represent more actual readers than 5,000 free downloads because buyers are far more likely to engage with what they paid for
Charging money signals quality and seriousness — her zine became a required university text and companies buy them for employees
She prices at $10 for individuals as a 'reasonable/fair' price rather than tying to business outcomes, while experimenting with higher corporate pricing
Posting draft pages on Twitter serves as both free access for those who can't pay and as feedback/marketing for the paid product
money_quotes: If you decide to sell your work instead of giving it away for free, you don't need to justify that.
I think 'this costs money' is a nice way to signal 'I actually spent time on this, this is good'.
Someone who bought a zine is probably like 4x more likely to read it than someone who just clicked on a PDF.
The fact that I'm making money from it makes it way easier to spend time on the project.
If I could figure out how to charge more, I could share that with other people and make a world where smaller creators could be more successful.
I worry a lot about making mistakes when selling things to folks, but once I write down the issues so far they all feel very resolvable.
$10/zine is not actually a lot of money, it's only sustainable for julia because julia has a big audience.
key_insight: Selling creative educational work rather than giving it away can actually increase genuine readership while funding higher quality output, because people engage more seriously with things they pay for.
